title: Leaky ReLU: Fixing the 'Dying ReLU' Problem
concept: Standard ReLU outputs exactly 0 for every negative input, so some neurons can get permanently stuck outputting 0 and stop learning — a problem called 'dying ReLU.' Leaky ReLU fixes this by allowing a small non-zero slope for negative inputs instead of a hard 0.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what makes Softmax different from Sigmoid when choosing between many categories?

options.text: Softmax's probabilities are all linked and sum to 100%, while Sigmoid scores each decision independently

exercises.explain: Right — Softmax links every category's probability together. Now let's rewind to ReLU and uncover a flaw nobody mentioned yet.

cards.title: When a neuron flatlines forever
cards.body: ReLU seemed perfect: fast, simple, the modern default. But it hides a quiet failure mode — a neuron can get stuck outputting 0 forever, no matter what input it receives, and simply stop learning. This is called the dying ReLU problem.

cards.title: Leaky ReLU: never fully shut off
cards.body: Leaky ReLU tweaks ReLU's rule: instead of squashing every negative input to a hard 0, it lets a small trickle through — multiplying negative inputs by a tiny slope (like 0.01) instead of zeroing them out. Positive inputs still pass through unchanged.

cards.title: A faucet that never fully closes
cards.body: Remember ReLU's one-way valve? A dying neuron is like a valve slammed shut and rusted stuck — no flow ever gets through again. Leaky ReLU is a valve that never fully closes: even at its tightest, a slow trickle still gets through, keeping the pipe from rusting shut.

cards.title: Running numbers through Leaky ReLU
cards.body: With a slope of 0.01: Input -5 → output -0.05 (a tiny trickle, not zero). Input 0 → output 0. Input 3 → output 3, same as regular ReLU. Only the negative side changed — from a hard wall to a gentle slope.

cards.title: A dead neuron doesn't just 'wake up'
cards.body: You might assume a neuron stuck at 0 will eventually recover once other weights shift. In reality, once a ReLU neuron's output stays 0 for every input it sees, no signal — no 'direction of steepest change' — ever flows back through it to update its weights. It's stuck for good, unless the function itself changes.

cards.title: Hard wall vs. small trickle
cards.body: How the two handle negative inputs:

left.text:
Negative inputs → exactly 0
Can get stuck and stop learning

right.text:
Negative inputs → tiny slope, not zero
Keeps a trickle of learning alive

exercises.prompt: What problem can happen when a ReLU neuron's inputs are always negative?

options.text: It gets permanently stuck outputting 0 and stops learning ('dying ReLU')
It automatically switches itself to Leaky ReLU
It outputs a random number instead

exercises.explain: Right — a ReLU neuron stuck at 0 for every input it ever sees stops learning entirely. That's the dying ReLU problem.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Leaky ReLU outputs exactly 0 for every negative input, just like regular ReLU.
exercises.explain: False! Leaky ReLU allows a small non-zero slope for negative inputs instead of a hard 0.

exercises.sentence: Leaky ReLU fixes the 'dying ___' problem by never fully zeroing out negative inputs.
exercises.explain: Leaky ReLU exists specifically to fix the dying ReLU problem.

exercises.prompt: A neuron receives an input of -8. Which activation function gives a small negative output instead of exactly 0?
exercises.explain: Leaky ReLU multiplies negative inputs by a small slope instead of zeroing them out completely.

exercises.prompt: A team notices that after training, a big chunk of their network's neurons always output exactly 0, no matter what input they're given — and their weights never seem to update anymore. What's happening, and which fix directly addresses it?

options.text: This is the dying ReLU problem — regular ReLU zeroes out negative inputs completely, and Leaky ReLU fixes it by keeping a small non-zero slope for negative inputs
This is normal and means the network has finished training perfectly
This means the network needs more layers, not a different activation function
This only happens with Softmax, never with ReLU

exercises.explain: Exactly! Leaky ReLU's small slope keeps a trickle of signal flowing even for negative inputs, preventing neurons from getting permanently stuck. But what if, instead of picking that slope by hand, the network could learn the best slope itself?
